content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 8,
1992
Honorable lila Collin
Supervisor,
Second District
Sacramento County
7423 Braeridge Way
Sacramento,
CA
95831
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—92—381
Dear Supervisor Collin:
This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to Joyce Mihanovich of your office on
June 23,
1992,
concerning the propriety of using your campaign
funds to contribute to a recall committee pursuant to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
This letter
confirms the advice that I provided to Ms. Mihanovich, that the
Act does riot restrict your ability to make a contribution to a
recall committee.
Under the “personal use” provisions of the Act, the
expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably related to a
political, legislative,
or governmental purpose.
2
(Section
89512.)
A contribution made to a recall committee would appear to
be political in nature.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
However,
where an expenditure confers a substantial personal
benefit on the candidate or other controlling person, the
expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose.
A “substantial personal benefit” is
defined as a direct personal benefit to the candidate of $100 or
more.
(Section 89511(b) (2).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-381
Page
2
In addition,
Section 85304 of the Act prohibits the transfer
of the campaign funds of one candidate to another candidate under
certain circumstances.
3
However, the transfer ban does not apply
to contributions from a candidate to a measure committee.
Recall
committees are classified as measure committees under Section
82043 of the Act.
(Benya Advice Letter,
No.
1—92—079.)
Consequently, the prohibition does not apply to a contribution to
a recall committee.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—590l.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
ing General Counsel
(QA
rohn W. Wallace
isel,
Legal Division
SH:JWW:aa
On September 25,
1990,
a United States District Court
invalidated the fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act,
and the transfer ban as applied to support such limits.
(Service
Employees International Union.
AFL-CIO,
CLC, et al. v. Fair
Political Practices Commission
(9th Cir.
1992)
955 F.2d 1312,
cert. den.
U.S.
The transfer ban of Section 85305
continues to apply where campaign funds are being transferred to a
candidate in a special election, or to a candidate subject to
local contribution limits.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

sections.conclusion: The Act does not restrict the ability to make a contribution to a recall committee. A contribution to a recall committee would be political in nature under the personal use provisions, and recall committees are classified as measure committees, so the campaign fund transfer ban does not apply.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a county supervisor may use campaign funds to contribute to a recall committee under the Political Reform Act's personal use and campaign fund transfer restrictions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirmed that campaign funds may be contributed to a recall committee because such contributions are political in nature and the transfer ban applicable to candidate-to-candidate transfers does not apply to contributions to measure committees, which includes recall committees.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a confirmation letter of prior telephone advice dated June 23, 1992. Document contains footnotes with statutory references and court case citations. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers, but the question and conclusion are clearly stated in the opening and main body paragraphs.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a county supervisor may use campaign funds to contribute to a recall committee under the Political Reform Act's personal use and campaign fund transfer restrictions.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not restrict the ability to make a contribution to a recall committee. A contribution to a recall committee would be political in nature under the personal use provisions, and recall committees are classified as measure committees, so the campaign fund transfer ban does not apply.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter confirming that a Sacramento County Supervisor may use campaign funds to contribute to a recall committee, as such contributions are political in nature and exempt from the campaign fund transfer restrictions of the Political Reform Act.
